name: The Dimmer — the harness never moves, only the light
oneLineEssence: The whole truck stays exactly where it is and the tech's eye is steered purely by light: the lift-pump circuit holds full ink while the rest of the fuel harness dims to a faint blueprint — a stage dimmer board, not a camera.
feltExperiencePitch: You open P0087 on the 6.7 Power Stroke and the whole fuel system is drawn on one calm sheet — PCM up top, the injectors and Bosch CP4.2 down the middle, the lift pump out on the driver-side frame rail. The walk lands you on "back-probe the lift pump power feed, key-on," and in about a quarter second the sheet just... settles: the lift pump, its relay, the 12V feed wire, the ground down to the chassis, and the rail-pressure sensor it's all chasing stay full strength and the current keeps flowing through the feed; everything else — the eight injectors, the CP4.2, the metering valve — fades back to a faint blueprint like someone pulled their stage lights down. Nothing slid, nothing zoomed, nothing reflowed, so your hand never loses the lift pump; it's right where it always was, just lit. You tap the lift pump connector and a small card lifts off the part with a hairline leader line still touching the pin, and the first thing it says is the number you're chasing — "Right now: 12V briefly during prime, then 0V / Should read 12V on command" — with where to put your probe right under it. The parts the tool is sure about are drawn in solid ink; the one fact it only knows from theory is drawn a shade lighter, so you can feel what's been confirmed versus inferred before you read a word. It feels like a precision instrument that turned to face the part you asked about — not a website with a diagram bolted in.

sheet sits at bottom; lift pump stays
lit ABOVE it (camera unmoved — it's a
fixed sheet, not an overlay on the part)
focusFadeMechanic: Layout is frozen forever: dagre positions (topology-layout.ts) never change and the React Flow viewport never auto-pans or auto-zooms — we keep the existing single `fitView` on mount only. Focus is a pure luminance/weight change driven by the walk's current step, not the camera. Add a `step` model alongside today's flat `TopologySelectionState`: each step declares a `focusNodeIds` set + a `focusEdgeIds` set (for the lift-pump-feed step: lift pump, lift pump relay, chassis frame ground, primary fuel filter, fuel tank, plus FRP sensor as a labeled context anchor; edges = the 12V relay→pump wire, the pump→ground wire, the tank/filter fluid lines, and FRP→PCM signal). On step entry, toFlowElements (topology-flow.ts) tags every node and edge with `data.recede: boolean`. The CSS does the rest: focused nodes/wires render at full ink; receded nodes get a new `.topo-node.is-recede { opacity: var(--vt-recede); }` (--vt-recede: 0.2 desktop, 0.13 mobile — never 0, never display:none) and receded wires reuse the EXISTING `.wire.off` floor (0.16, animation:none) so their dash-flow FREEZES while focused wires keep animating at true electrical-state speed (the 12V feed = `.wire.steady-12v` flow). The whole settle is ONE coordinated transition: `transition: opacity var(--vt-dur-3) var(--vt-ease-instrument)` (280ms, the calm no-bounce curve) on nodes, wires, and labels together, so the eye reads it as the room dimming, not N elements blinking. Critically, the fade is a PERSISTENT MASK (Altium "Apply Mask During Interactive Editing"): it is keyed only to the step, so tapping parts WITHIN a step never re-resolves the dim — the field stays stable; it re-settles only when the walk advances. Because the only motion is opacity, this mode IS the prefers-reduced-motion-safe default (opacity fades are the WCAG-2.3.3 safe substitute) — there is no separate degradation branch to build; under reduced-motion we simply drop the wire dash-flow (already gated in topology.css) and the opacity settle stays.
tapToPullBehavior: Tapping the Electric Lift Pump (or its 12V pin) on pointerdown immediately raises a small part-anchored detail card — not the current detached right `<aside>`, and never a standing panel. The card is positioned by React Flow's node screen-coordinate (we already have it) and is connected to the actual pin by a 1px hairline LEADER LINE (Figma measurement-feedback style) so it reads as belonging to that part, not floating beside the workspace. It anchors to a STABLE spot (Figma UI3 lesson: never pin a popover to a moving node) — to the right of the focused cluster on desktop, never on top of the part being worked. The tap does NOT move the camera and does NOT re-resolve the fade (selection ≠ navigation, the Figma Shift+2 rule). Content is ordered by the moment (see stepAwareOrdering). On dismiss (tap the diagram pane → existing onPaneClick) the card settles away over --vt-dur-2. Tapping a FADED part is first-class: it pulls that part's card the same way AND briefly lifts only that one part from the 0.2 recede floor to ~0.55 ("peek") so the tech sees what they touched, without changing the step or disturbing the rest of the mask — it settles back when the card closes. This lets a tech sanity-check "is the CP4.2 downstream of this?" mid-step without leaving the lift-pump focus.
stepAwareOrdering: The SAME tap on the lift-pump connector is ordered differently by what the walk is doing. Mid-test on the 12V feed (step type PROBE/INTERPRET), the card LEADS with the reading for THIS test, drawn as an instrument value, not prose: a "RIGHT NOW" line bound to the active scenario — for Key On · Engine Off that is the real seed value "12V briefly during prime, then 0V" (wire-state pwm-low) — set directly against the expected reference "should read 12V on command (PCM: prime, crank, or run)" so a wrong number reads as wrong at a glance (PicoScope expected-vs-actual). Immediately under it, the one action: "PROBE: back-probe the power wire at the lift pump connector, under the truck near the tank." Everything architectural is demoted below the fold, dimmer, one level down and pulled only on further tap: connector + "2 wires — one power, one ground", what each wire does, location, then the short Operational Theori ("Two-wire electric pump in the tank. PCM commands it on. A relay or driver module sits upstream..."). The failure tree (pin.missingLogic: "0V when commanded → probe upstream: fuel pump relay, fuse, driver module; 12V but pump silent → ground side or seized motor") stays COLLAPSED until the observed reading is actually out of range — never pre-loaded onto a passing probe. If the same lift-pump tap happened during a LOCATE step, the card would instead lead with location ("driver-side frame rail") and suppress the reading; during ORIENT it would lead with the one-line role. Connector/wires/Operational Theori are always secondary, never the lead, never a theory-row dump.
provenanceTreatment: Provenance is felt before it is read, because it is drawn into the ink, not stamped as a badge (the rejected verdict-pill is structurally impossible here). On the canvas: FIELD-VERIFIED parts/wires draw at full ink weight; TRAINING-INFERRED draws at a slightly lighter/desaturated stroke; GAP draws with a hairline DASHED edge (the universal "provisional / not-to-scale" convention from real schematics) — so the tool literally draws less boldly where it knows less. In this scene the lift pump itself, its 12V/GND pins, the relay→pump wire, and the expected "12V" reading are all TRAINING-CONFIRMED, so they read at full confidence; the lift-pump-relay's exact BJB cavity and the FRP regulator are softer, and any true GAP (e.g. an unconfirmed driver module) carries the hairline dashed edge. In the detail card, the source grade is one quiet mono caption directly under the value it qualifies — "inferred from theory" in neutral graphite (--vt-fg-3, the default, no color), amber "needs field check" (--vt-amber-500) ONLY for a GAP the tech is about to act on (ISO-2575 amber = advisory, the one state allowed to raise salience), "field-verified" a single quiet navy tick. Amber is reserved strictly for gaps and red strictly for an actual measured-wrong fault reading, so the two colors never blur. The caption text IS the see-source affordance (no separate sources column).
scenarioStateTreatment: Scenario state lives WITHIN the canvas as a thin recessed reading, never a competing block. The current ScenarioBar (4 horizontal groups: Ignition/Engine/Load/Fault) collapses to ONE low-contrast mono line just under the title ribbon: "now showing · KEY ON · ENGINE OFF ▾ · lift pump primes briefly, then stops" — drawn in --vt-fg-3 so it is orientation chrome, not a center-of-gravity (Linear: recede the chrome by luminance). The scenario name is a quiet dropdown affordance; tapping the ▾ opens a small in-canvas scenario picker (the eight real scenarios: Key Off, Key On·Engine Off, Engine Idle [default], Light/Medium/Heavy Load, and the two Fault Sims), which closes back out of the way on pick. The live "right now" value never sits on top of the part — it appears both as the recessed reading line AND, when a part is tapped, as the leading instrument value in the card. When the active scenario changes, ONLY the wire-flow states and the readings re-render (the fade mask is keyed to the step, not the scenario, so changing scenario does not re-dim the harness) — the focused 12V feed wire simply changes its flow speed/state to match (e.g. pwm-low on Key-On prime vs steady-12v at idle), which is itself a felt cue that the scenario changed.
seeSourcePath: The provenance caption under each value is the affordance — there is no standing "sources" column. Tapping "inferred from theory" (or the amber "needs field check") expands a short why-we-believe-this inline, in place, below the caption — the same pull-not-push grammar as the part detail itself: it answers in one or two lines (what the draft is inferred from, what a field check would confirm) and collapses again on a second tap. It is short, every-word-counts, never a theory-row dump. On desktop it expands within the card; on mobile it expands within the bottom sheet's expanded detent. The affordance is a >=44px tap target on touch. This keeps "evidence, not oracle" true without the word "AI" ever appearing and without any badge wall.
visualLanguage: Reuse the in-repo "Workshop Instrument" tokens wholesale (app/globals.css) — do NOT mint a parallel palette; the canvas should be indistinguishable from the curator desktop kit. Surface: bone ramp (--vt-bg = --vt-bone-50). REMOVE React Flow's default dotted `<Background />` and default `<Controls />` and `colorMode="light"` chrome (the rejected default-AI look, confirmed present in topology-diagram.tsx line 111-114) — if any ground is wanted, use the near-invisible OKLCH graph-paper texture already in the vt kit, not a second decorative grid. THE HIGHEST-LEVERAGE ADD: define the six `--role-*` tokens that topology.css references on every `.wire--*` rule but that exist NOWHERE today (so wires currently render colorless) — map them onto the element ramp: --role-12v → warm red near --vt-risk-high (power = hot), --role-ground → --vt-fg-2 graphite (return = neutral), --role-signal → --vt-elem-voltage chartreuse, --role-5v-ref → --vt-elem-coolant cyan, --role-low-ref → desaturated coolant, --role-pwm → --vt-elem-vacuum violet. Add --vt-recede: 0.2 as the canonical faded floor. Type: mono+tnum (JetBrains Mono, font-feature-settings:'tnum') for EVERY reading/number/pin (the "12V briefly, then 0V" value, the expected band, the <0.5Ω ground spec) so digits never jitter; Instrument Serif for the part name and the one-line Operational Theori prose; Inter Tight micro-caps for eyebrows/labels only. Replace the heavy 1.4px-bordered .topo-node card with an engraved faceplate: 0.5px hairline (matching every vt divider), serif name, mono micro-cap kind/location, pins as the only high-contrast geometry on the plate; selection = navy ink halo + --vt-accent-bg tint (NOT the current 2px amber border, which collides with amber=caution). Radii stay small (--vt-radius-1/2). Wire is the active circuit's hero: a 1px bone-50 casing underlay (Mapbox route-casing) + the existing .wire.is-active 3.5px glow on the focused 12V feed so it separates cleanly from faded neighbors.
appWidePropagation: Once the canvas proves luminance-as-hierarchy, the same language propagates app-wide cheaply because it is built on tokens the rest of the app already shares. The six newly-defined --role-* tokens become the canonical wire/electrical-role palette everywhere a circuit is drawn (curator preview, any future wiring reference), killing the colorless-wire bug repo-wide. --vt-recede: 0.2 becomes the standard "this supports orientation, it recedes" opacity used wherever the app separates the active task from its context (Linear's nav-dimming applied broadly) — e.g. dimming non-active rows in curator lists rather than adding borders. Provenance-as-ink-weight (full/lighter/dashed + the quiet mono caption that is itself the see-source link) becomes the house style for ALL drafted-vs-verified content, so trust reads consistently and no surface ever grows a badge wall. The engraved-faceplate node treatment and the part-anchored leader-line detail card give the app a reusable "instrument plate + pulled detail" pattern for any spatial/diagram surface. And the --vt-dur-3 / --vt-ease-instrument calm-settle becomes the standard transition for any focus change, so the whole product moves like one precise instrument.
motionSpec: Exactly one motion vocabulary, and it does diagnostic work only (the OP-1 test — no decoration). STEP SETTLE: when the walk advances, every node/wire/label cross-fades to its new ink/recede level over --vt-dur-3 (280ms) on --vt-ease-instrument (cubic-bezier(.4,0,.2,1)) — one coordinated opacity-only transition, no scale-pop, no reflow, no camera move; reads as a dimmer board easing down (damped like a gauge needle, no overshoot). TAP COMMIT: on pointerdown the tapped part gets a single decisive state change — navy ink halo + --vt-accent-bg tint fading in over --vt-dur-1 (80ms) plus a restrained ~1.02 scale (Things-3-confident, not web-hover-soft) so the canvas reads as turning to face the part the tech declared intent on; the detail card lifts over --vt-dur-2 (160ms) with the leader line drawing in. PEEK (faded-part tap): that one part eases 0.2 → ~0.55 over --vt-dur-2 and back on close. WIRE FLOW: the focused 12V feed keeps its true-state dash-flow (steady-12v = flow 2.4s, or pwm-low on key-on prime); receded wires freeze (animation:none via the existing .wire.off). REDUCED MOTION: because the only structural motion is opacity, the focus model needs no special path — under prefers-reduced-motion we drop the tap scale and the wire dash-flow (already gated) and keep the opacity settle, which is the WCAG-safe substitute. Total perceived focus time on step change: ~280ms, no blink, the sheet never goes blank.
citations: KiCad net highlighting — highlighted net bright, all other items dimmer (fade-the-rest as established pro-EDA focus, no camera): https://docs.kicad.org/9.0/en/pcbnew/pcbnew.html

Altium Designer Cross-Select Mode + 'Apply Mask During Interactive Editing' — dim/mask non-selected, mask persists during work so focus doesn't flicker: https://www.altium.com/documentation/altium-designer/sch-pcb/cross-probing-selecting

Linear 2026 design refresh — orientation/nav chrome rendered a few notches dimmer so the work area takes precedence; hierarchy by luminance, 'don't compete for attention you haven't earned': https://linear.app/now/behind-the-latest-design-refresh

Snap-on ZEUS Guided Component Tests — locate → connector/pin config → normal-operation context → live meter pulled on demand (our Operational Theori = ZEUS 'normal operation'): https://www.snapon.com/DiagnosticsManuals/ZEUS%20NA/Content/ZEUS/ComponentTest1.htm

PicoScope Automotive reference waveforms — expected reference fixed on-screen against the live capture (expected-vs-actual as one glance): https://www.picoauto.com/library/picoscope-automotive-training/knowledge-base/reference-waveforms

Mapbox Navigation route-line casing layer — wide underlay under the main line so the active route separates from context; inactive legs styled independently: https://docs.mapbox.com/android/navigation/v2/guides/ui-components/route-line/

Figma 'Zoom to Selection' (Shift+2) — selection does NOT move the viewport; only an explicit command reframes (selection ≠ navigation): https://help.figma.com/hc/en-us/articles/360040328653-Use-Figma-products-with-a-keyboard

Figma UI3 floating-toolbar backlash — don't pin a contextual surface to a moving target / in the way of the work (anti-pattern guard for the detail card anchor): https://forum.figma.com/share-your-feedback-26/ui3-remove-floating-center-bar-38978

MDN/web.dev prefers-reduced-motion — opacity fades are the WCAG 2.3.3 safe substitute for pan/zoom, so an opacity-only focus model is reduced-motion-native: https://web.dev/articles/prefers-reduced-motion

ISO 2575 / SAE J578 telltale color hierarchy — amber/yellow = advisory caution, red = stop-now; grounds amber 'needs field check' vs red fault reading: https://blog.ansi.org/ansi/sae-j-578-2020-color-lighting-motor-vehicles/

ISS Automotive on cluster needle damping — a trustworthy gauge settles smoothly with no flutter; grounds the damped 280ms settle vs a hard opacity flip: https://issautomotive.com/blogs/instrument-cluster/cluster-needles
React Flow node/edge data + class styling (the in-stack primitive: tag nodes/edges with data.recede, style via CSS — no new layout/camera code): https://reactflow.dev/api-reference/types/node
risks: The signature risk of NOT moving the camera: on a dense harness a small bright lift-pump cluster can be hard to FIND in a large static frame, especially on desktop where the receded harness fills the viewport. Mitigation: the 1px bone casing + 3.5px is-active glow on the focused wires, the engraved-plate contrast, and an always-present 'show whole system' affordance — but this is the bet (spatial memory of a fixed truck beats being driven) and must be validated against the real ~25-node fuel graph, not a toy.

Recede tuning is delicate: 0.2 may be too faint on a bright bay phone screen (faded parts vanish, breaking 'fade not vanish' and losing orientation) or too strong (the dim doesn't read as 'not now'). Needs on-device calibration; mobile uses 0.13 + label suppression which pushes harder toward vanish — watch that line.

Provenance-as-ink-weight is subtle by design, which is also its risk: a lighter stroke for 'inferred from theory' may be imperceptible at a glance or be misread as 'less important' rather than 'less certain'. The mono caption carries the real meaning; the ink-weight is reinforcement, and we must verify the lighter weight is distinguishable from the recede dim (two different 'lightnesses' on screen at once).

The part-anchored card with a leader line is harder than a fixed aside: it must never cover the focused part, must reposition if the part is near a viewport edge, and the leader line must track if the tech manually pans/zooms. Getting this wrong re-creates the Figma-UI3 'in the way of the work' failure.

Curator-only 'X captured / Y missing' must be gated server-side by role, not just hidden with CSS — a technician/owner must never receive it. The current CapturedMissingFooter overlays the canvas; it has to move behind a role gate and out of the technician canvas entirely.

No automatic reframe means a tech who pans away to inspect a faded part has no one-gesture 'recenter on the step' — needs a cheap 'back to the step' affordance or the stillness becomes a place-losing liability, the inverse of the camera direction's strobing risk.

The step model itself is net-new product surface (focusNodeIds/focusEdgeIds per step) and depends on a walk/step source that does not exist in topology-diagnostic.tsx today (selection is a flat explorer); this direction assumes that wizard-step feed is built, which is a real upstream dependency, not pure styling.
buildNotes: Mostly additive on top of the existing @xyflow/react stack — the heavy lifting is CSS + a data flag, not a new engine. (1) DEFINE the six --role-* tokens in app/globals.css :root (today they are referenced by topology.css on every .wire--* rule but defined nowhere, so wires render colorless — this is a standing bug; fixing it is step one). Add --vt-recede: 0.2. (2) topology-diagram.tsx: drop `<Background />`, drop `<Controls />` (or replace with a single 'show whole system' button + token-styled fit), remove colorMode='light'. Keep the single mount-time fitView; never call fitView on step change or tap. (3) Add a `step` to the props/state next to TopologySelectionState — shape { focusNodeIds: Set<string>, focusEdgeIds: Set<string>, stepType: 'orient'|'locate'|'probe'|'interpret'|'branch', leadFact }. This is the real upstream dependency: it needs the guided-wizard step feed, which does not exist in the screen today (selection is the flat empty|component|pin|connection explorer). (4) topology-flow.ts (toFlowElements): tag each node/edge with data.recede = !focusNodeIds.has(id). (5) topology-node.tsx + topology.css: add `.topo-node.is-recede { opacity: var(--vt-recede); }` and a matching edge class; reuse the existing .wire.off freeze for receded wires and .wire.is-active glow for focused wires; add the one coordinated `transition: opacity var(--vt-dur-3) var(--vt-ease-instrument)`. Replace the heavy node border with the 0.5px engraved-plate treatment; change selection from 2px amber to navy halo + --vt-accent-bg. (6) Replace the detached right `<aside>` (topology-detail-panel.tsx) with a part-anchored card positioned from the node's React Flow screen coords + an SVG hairline leader line to the pin; reorder content to lead with the step-relevant reading (PinBody already has scenario.pinReadings + pin.expectedReading + pin.missingLogic — reorder, don't refetch; all data is in-memory, zero tap-time network). (7) Provenance: render the ink-weight on the node (data already carries sourceProvenance per component/pin/connection) + the mono caption that toggles an inline see-source expansion. (8) Mobile: card becomes a non-modal bottom sheet (peek/expand detents), --vt-recede tightened to 0.13 + label suppression on receded nodes, pin hit-targets padded to >=44px. No new dependency; reduced-motion needs no special branch because the focus is opacity-only.

check.direction: The Dimmer — the harness never moves, only the light

spineCompliance.diagramIsWorkspace: PASS. Removes React Flow's default <Background/>, <Controls/>, and colorMode='light' (all confirmed present at topology-diagram.tsx lines 110-114) and gives the canvas the full sheet. Context (scenario reading line, detail card) lives within the canvas, not in competing rails. The current .topo grid hands 380px to a fixed right panel and stacks scenario/readout above the canvas (topology.css lines 14-38); this direction collapses all of that into the canvas, which is the strongest possible reading of spine point 1.
spineCompliance.stepAwareOnlyWhatsNeeded: PASS in intent, but PROVISIONAL on a dependency. The step model (focusNodeIds/focusEdgeIds + stepType) correctly drives which parts hold ink vs recede, and the lift-pump focus set (pump, relay, chassis ground, filter, tank, FRP-as-context) matches the representative scene. BUT this requires a guided-wizard step feed that does NOT exist today — topology-flow.ts confirms selection is the flat empty|component|connection|pin explorer with zero step awareness. The direction is honest that this is the real upstream dependency, not styling. Compliant as a design; inert until the feed exists.
spineCompliance.fadeNotVanish: PASS with a caution. Recede is opacity to a floor (--vt-recede: 0.2 desktop / 0.13 mobile), explicitly never 0 / never display:none, and receded wires reuse the existing .wire.off floor (0.16) rather than disappearing (topology.css line 290). This is a faithful read of 'fade, not vanish.' The caution: 0.13 + label suppression on mobile pushes toward vanish on a bright bay screen — an unvalidated number on the exact gate that matters most.
spineCompliance.tapToPullStepAware: PASS, and the strongest part of the direction. Tap raises a part-anchored card on pointerdown (pull = tech declaring intent), never a standing panel; it leads with the step-relevant fact (the RIGHT NOW reading mid-PROBE, location mid-LOCATE, role mid-ORIENT) and demotes connector/wires/Operational Theori below the fold. Critically, tap does NOT re-resolve the fade (Figma Shift+2: selection != navigation), so the field stays stable. The failure tree (pin.missingLogic) stays collapsed until a reading is actually out of range — a clean read of 'every word counts, no pre-loaded theory dump.' All data is in-memory (PinBody already reads scenario.pinReadings + pin.expectedReading + pin.missingLogic), so it is genuinely a reorder, not a refetch.
spineCompliance.contextWithinCanvas: PASS. Scenario state collapses from the 4-group horizontal ScenarioBar to one low-contrast mono line under the title; the live reading appears as the recessed line AND as the leading card value, never on top of the worked part; the card anchors to a stable spot to the side of the focused cluster, never over it. This is exactly spine point 5.
spineCompliance.curatorOnlyCompleteness: PASS in stated intent, FLAGGED in current code. The direction explicitly moves the 'X captured / Y missing' behind a server-side role gate and out of the technician canvas. This is necessary because today CapturedMissingFooter renders unconditionally in topology-diagnostic.tsx (line 189, no role check) and overlays the canvas. The direction correctly names this in its risk list and treats it as a build requirement; must be enforced server-side, not CSS-hidden.

guardrailCompliance.noAiWord: PASS. No 'AI' word anywhere in copy. Provenance framed around the source ('inferred from theory') and the action ('needs field check'), the model stays invisible plumbing. Matches the existing PROVENANCE_LABEL map and the no-AI-word constraint.
guardrailCompliance.noStepNofM: PASS. No 'step N of M', no upcoming-work preview, no visible stepper. The walk advances silently and the canvas re-settles; the stepType drives content ordering but is never surfaced as a counter. The direction explicitly leans on 'show only done + now' — there is no progress chrome at all.
guardrailCompliance.mobilePasses: PARTIAL / UNPROVEN. A real degradation is specified (non-modal bottom sheet with peek/expand detents, camera-unmoved with the lift pump lit above the sheet, >=44px pin targets — an upgrade over today's 32px floor at topology.css line 341, 28px min-height). But the central mobile move (recede to 0.13 + label suppression on receded nodes) is the riskiest fade-not-vanish line and is asserted, not calibrated on a 375-414px device. Cannot be called a confirmed pass until eyeballed on-device.
guardrailCompliance.provenanceHonest: FAIL as written — fixable. This is the one real violation. The schema carries FOUR provenance values (TRAINING-CONFIRMED, TRAINING-INFERRED, FIELD-VERIFIED, GAP — lib/db/schema.ts; existing label map in topology-detail-panel.tsx lines 62-67). The direction draws BOTH TRAINING-CONFIRMED and FIELD-VERIFIED at the SAME 'full ink / full confidence' weight, so a tech cannot see the difference between 'the model is sure from theory' and 'a human verified this on a truck.' That collapses the exact distinction the trust model protects ('never says confirmed until a human field-verifies'). The mono caption still differentiates the words, but the felt-before-read ink channel actively lies. Needs a distinct fourth ink weight.
guardrailCompliance.notDefaultAiAesthetic: PASS, decisively. Explicitly kills the rejected default-AI tells: removes the dotted Background, removes default Controls, drops the 1.4px node border for a 0.5px engraved faceplate, replaces the 2px amber selection border (topology.css lines 97-100) with a navy ink halo (also fixing an amber=caution color collision), and reuses the in-repo Workshop Instrument tokens wholesale instead of minting a parallel palette. Provenance-as-ink-weight makes the rejected verdict-pill structurally impossible. Citations are real and load-bearing (KiCad net highlight, Altium Apply Mask, Linear luminance, Snap-on ZEUS, PicoScope expected-vs-actual, Mapbox route casing, Figma Shift+2 / UI3, ISO 2575 amber, WCAG 2.3.3).

principleViolations: Provenance honesty: TRAINING-CONFIRMED and FIELD-VERIFIED are both drawn at identical 'full ink' weight, erasing the visual difference between theory-grade confidence and human field verification — the core distinction the trust model exists to protect. Real violation, but fixable with a fourth ink weight; not disqualifying.

Curator-only completeness is correct in the direction's stated intent but the current CapturedMissingFooter is rendered with no role gate (topology-diagnostic.tsx line 189). The direction names this as a fix-required item, so it is a carried debt to enforce, not a violation of the direction itself.
check.distinctnessVsOthers: Genuinely distinct, and the cleanest articulation of one axis of the space. The Dimmer's whole thesis is the negative space of The Drive: 'no camera ever moves,' betting that spatial memory of a fixed truck beats being driven turn-by-turn. The Meter makes the reading the hero and the diagram a backdrop; The Dimmer keeps the diagram at full structural authority and steers by luminance only. The distinctness risk is at the TAP layer, not the FOCUS layer — all three converge on a part-anchored card that leads with the step-relevant reading, so once a part is tapped they look similar. The Dimmer stays differentiated because its tap is explicitly non-navigational (Figma Shift+2: selection never re-resolves the fade or moves anything). That 'nothing moves, ever' discipline is a defensible identity, not a reskin of a sibling.
honestRisks: The provenance collision above is the most serious finding: equal ink for TRAINING-CONFIRMED and FIELD-VERIFIED lets theory-confidence borrow field-verified authority on the trust-first surface.

The signature bet (no camera move) is the signature risk and the direction is honest about it: on the real ~25-node fuel graph a small bright lift-pump cluster can be hard to FIND in a large static frame, especially desktop where the faded harness fills the viewport and the single mount-time fitView never reframes. Mitigations (casing + is-active glow + 'show whole system') are real but unproven at scale.

'No automatic reframe' means a tech who pans to inspect a faded part has no one-gesture recenter; the proposed 'back to the step' affordance is named but under-specified, and without it stillness becomes a place-losing liability (the inverse of The Drive's strobing).
Recede floors (0.2 / 0.13) are asserted, not calibrated; 0.13 + mobile label suppression is the riskiest fade-not-vanish line and lives on the hard mobile gate.

Two simultaneous on-screen 'lightnesses' — recede dim (orientation) vs provenance ink-weight (certainty) — can be confused; a faded-but-field-verified part vs a full-ink-but-inferred part is easy to get wrong and hard to catch in review.

The step model is net-new surface depending on a guided-wizard step feed that does not exist in topology-diagnostic.tsx today (flat selection only); the direction is inert until that feed ships. Shared by all siblings but real.

The part-anchored leader-line card is harder than the current detached aside: must never cover the focused part, must reposition at viewport edges, and the leader line must track manual pan/zoom or it re-creates the Figma-UI3 'in the way of the work' failure it cites against.
suggestedFixes: Fix the provenance collision: define FOUR ink weights, not three. TRAINING-INFERRED = lighter/desaturated stroke; TRAINING-CONFIRMED = a distinct MID weight (clearly not equal to field-verified); FIELD-VERIFIED = full ink + quiet navy tick; GAP = hairline dashed + amber. Reuse the existing PROVENANCE_LABEL strings verbatim ('from theory' / 'inferred from theory' / 'field-verified' / 'needs field verification') so copy stays consistent and no new wording is invented.

Make the two lightness channels unmistakably orthogonal: recede = luminance/contrast drop on otherwise-normal ink; provenance = stroke weight + dash + mono caption, never opacity. State it as a rule so an inferred-but-focused part can never look like a faded part.

Fully specify the 'back to the step' recenter affordance — it is load-bearing precisely because the camera never auto-moves. Define the gesture, its in-canvas placement, and that it re-runs the single fitView scoped to the focus set.

Resolve the 'find the bright cluster in a static frame' risk concretely before sign-off: either (a) keep the no-camera bet but commit to validating recede floors + glow contrast on the real ~25-node graph, or (b) allow a ONE-TIME gentle fit-to-focus on step ENTRY only (never on tap) as a fallback — flag (b) to Brandon as a softening of the 'camera never moves' purity, not a silent change.
Calibrate --vt-recede on a real 375-414px device before claiming the mobile gate passes; 0.13 + label suppression is the line most likely to break fade-not-vanish.

Enforce the curator-only footer gate SERVER-SIDE and remove it from the technician canvas entirely — today CapturedMissingFooter renders unconditionally (topology-diagnostic.tsx line 189); CSS-hiding is insufficient.

Keep the standing-bug fix as step one: define the six --role-* tokens in app/globals.css (confirmed referenced by every .wire--* rule in topology.css lines 283-288 but defined nowhere, so wires render colorless today) and add --vt-recede. This is correct and low-risk.
